title: Macquarie closing on $3bn deal for Vic land registry
full_text:
The sale of Aware Super’s Secure Electronic Registries Victoria is heading towards bilateral negotiations with Macquarie Asset Management, say sources.
It comes as the competitive field for the $3bn-plus asset has thinned to the point where a direct deal looks the most likely outcome.
Macquarie was the underbidder when Aware Super – then known as First State Super – acquired the 40-year concession to operate Victoria’s land titles registry in 2018 for more than $2.86bn. It already owns South Australia’s land registry alongside Canada’s PSP Investments, giving it both the operational blueprint and the appetite for another long-dated, regulated cash flow asset of this type.
The path to a bilateral deal has not been straightforward and a federal budget complication has also weighed on the process. The government’s proposed negative gearing changes, restricting deductions on established residential purchases, raised genuine valuation uncertainty given that SERV’s earnings are directly tied to Victorian property transaction volumes.
For buyers writing cheques at 19 to 25 times earnings, even a modest softening in volumes feeds directly into the model.
That complexity, combined with a thinning field, appears to have accelerated the move towards a direct negotiation.
SERV generates about $160m in earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation annually, processing more than one million property transactions using automation and cloud technology.
At the expected valuation range, the implied price sits between roughly $3bn and $4bn – well within Macquarie’s capacity and consistent with comparable registry transactions globally. The process is being run by Barrenjoey on behalf of Aware Super.
